introSection: Hercules was a wooden roller coaster previously located at Dorney Park & Wildwater Kingdom in Allentown, Pennsylvania, USA. It operated from May 6, 1989 to September 1, 2003. The ride was built by Dinn Corporation and designed by Curtis D. Summers.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
On July 15, 1988, Dorney Park & Wildwater Kingdom announced that they would be installing a new wooden roller coaster.

When Hercules opened on May 6, 1989, it was advertised as being the "World's Tallest Wooden Roller Coaster". This was not true, but it did have the tallest drop on a wooden roller coaster. Following the opening of Mean Streak at Cedar Point in May 1991, Dorney Park disputed Cedar Point's claim that Mean Streak had the tallest drop on a wooden roller coaster. The park claimed that Hercules had a tall drop,  taller than Mean Streak's. Designer Curtis D. Summers subsequently said Hercules' drop was actually  tall and thus Mean Streak would indeed have the tallest drop on a wooden roller coaster at  when it opened in 1991.

The ride closed September 1, 2003 after years of dwindling popularity, and guests surveys showed the coaster to be the lowest rated in the park. Hercules also stretched to the park's boundaries, which sometimes annoyed the nearby neighbors. Deconstruction began in November of 2003, and by 2004, the coaster was all gone. However, five concrete footers can be seen in the lake as of right now, where its scenic banked turn once sat.

In Greek mythology, Hercules slays the beast Hydra. However, Hydra The Revenge, a Bolliger & Mabillard floorless roller coaster replaced Hercules in 2005.

sections.title: Design
sections.content: The ride had a terrain track layout.

sections.title: Trains
sections.content: The trains were built by Philadelphia Toboggan Coasters, Inc.

fullText:
Hercules was a wooden roller coaster previously located at Dorney Park & Wildwater Kingdom in Allentown, Pennsylvania, USA. It operated from May 6, 1989 to September 1, 2003. The ride was built by Dinn Corporation and designed by Curtis D. Summers.

On July 15, 1988, Dorney Park & Wildwater Kingdom announced that they would be installing a new wooden roller coaster.

When Hercules opened on May 6, 1989, it was advertised as being the "World's Tallest Wooden Roller Coaster". This was not true, but it did have the tallest drop on a wooden roller coaster. Following the opening of Mean Streak at Cedar Point in May 1991, Dorney Park disputed Cedar Point's claim that Mean Streak had the tallest drop on a wooden roller coaster. The park claimed that Hercules had a tall drop,  taller than Mean Streak's. Designer Curtis D. Summers subsequently said Hercules' drop was actually  tall and thus Mean Streak would indeed have the tallest drop on a wooden roller coaster at  when it opened in 1991.

The ride closed September 1, 2003 after years of dwindling popularity, and guests surveys showed the coaster to be the lowest rated in the park. Hercules also stretched to the park's boundaries, which sometimes annoyed the nearby neighbors. Deconstruction began in November of 2003, and by 2004, the coaster was all gone. However, five concrete footers can be seen in the lake as of right now, where its scenic banked turn once sat.

In Greek mythology, Hercules slays the beast Hydra. However, Hydra The Revenge, a Bolliger & Mabillard floorless roller coaster replaced Hercules in 2005.

The ride had a terrain track layout.

The trains were built by Philadelphia Toboggan Coasters, Inc.
